solution: As \( x \to \infty \), only the highest-degree terms matter: the limit is the ratio of the leading coefficients, \( \dfrac{[[a]]}{[[d]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the coefficients of the linear (subleading) terms instead of the leading coefficients.
This inverts the ratio of leading coefficients.
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign.
This uses the constant terms instead of the leading coefficients.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.

solution: The denominator has HIGHER degree than the numerator, so dividing every term by the denominator's leading power \( x^2 \) sends every term to \( 0 \): the limit is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This wrongly treats numerator and denominator as having equal degree and takes a leading-coefficient ratio.
This uses the subleading coefficients as if forming a leading-coefficient ratio.
This uses the constant terms as if forming a ratio.
This reports the numerator's leading coefficient itself instead of recognizing the limit is 0.
This assumes the limit is 1, a common but unjustified default guess.
This assumes the limit is −1, a common but unjustified default guess.

solution: Numerator and denominator have the SAME (odd) degree \( 3 \), so as with \( x \to \infty \), the \( x^3 \) terms dominate and their sign contributions cancel identically in the ratio: the limit is still just the ratio of leading coefficients, \( \dfrac{[[a]]}{[[d]]} = [[answer]] \) — the direction of infinity does not flip the sign here.
distractor_rationales: This wrongly flips the sign, assuming \( x \to -\infty \) negates the equal-degree leading-coefficient ratio.
This uses the subleading coefficients instead of the leading coefficients.
This inverts the ratio of leading coefficients.
This uses the constant and subleading coefficients instead of the leading coefficients.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.

solution: The first term \( \dfrac{[[a]]}{x} \to 0 \), and the second term's numerator and denominator have equal degree, so it tends to the ratio of leading coefficients \( \dfrac{[[b]]}{[[e]]} \). The whole limit is \( 0 + \dfrac{[[b]]}{[[e]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses \( a \) (from the vanishing term) in place of the ratio \( b/e \).
This uses the constant terms \( c/d \) instead of the leading-coefficient ratio \( b/e \).
This wrongly keeps the vanishing term \( a \) as a nonzero contribution added to \( b/e \).
This mismatches the leading coefficient \( b \) with the wrong constant \( d \).
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
